dailyPhases.warmUp: Table with t: 0, 0.5, 1.0, 1.5, 2.0, 2.5, 3.0 and h(t): 6, 30, 42, 42, 30, 6, -30. Ask: 'As t increases from 0 to 1.5, what happens to h(t)? As t increases from 2.0 to 3.0?'
dailyPhases.topicIntroduction: Introduce covariation: two quantities changing together. Define function as each input mapped to exactly one output. Vocabulary: independent variable (domain), dependent variable (range), increasing, decreasing, constant. Four representations: graphical, analytical, numerical, verbal.
dailyPhases.scaffoldedExamples: Passwater Examples 1-4: Example 1 (empty vase with water at constant rate, match to four graph choices). Example 2 (piecewise function g on [-4, 6], identify increasing intervals). Example 3 (graph of f with dashed midline, five points A-E ‚Äî on interval B to C, is f positive/negative and increasing/decreasing?). Example 4 (same graph, interval A to B).
dailyPhases.guidedPractice: Students use sentence frames: 'As x increases from ___ to ___, y increases/decreases/remains constant by approximately ___.' Apply to temperature table: T(h) at hours 6, 8, 10, 12, 14, 16, 18.
dailyPhases.independentPractice: Given table of p(x) at x = 1, 3, 5, 7, 9: identify intervals where p is increasing, decreasing, or constant. Over which interval does p show the greatest rate of change? Justify.
dailyPhases.exitEvidence: Table of m(x) at x = 0, 2, 4, 6, 8. Identify one interval where m is increasing and one where m is decreasing. Describe tandem change on [0, 4] using sentence frame.
dailyPhases.capReflection: What persistence move did you make when the pattern was unclear?

dailyPhases.warmUp: Review: 'What is the critical difference between saying f is increasing and saying f is positive?' Recall Passwater's key note: these are VERY different statements.
dailyPhases.topicIntroduction: Review covariation language. Introduce rate of change sign on intervals. Critical distinction: 'f is increasing' describes direction of output, NOT position. A function can be increasing while negative. Always use intervals with the input variable.
dailyPhases.scaffoldedExamples: Passwater Examples 5-8: Example 5 (graph with six labeled points A-F ‚Äî 'On which interval is f negative and decreasing?'). Examples 6-8: multiple-choice on rate of change being positive/negative, increasing/decreasing, concavity on various intervals. AP note: FRQ3 will appear exactly like Example 3.
dailyPhases.guidedPractice: Students analyze a new graph with midline and labeled points. For each interval, state whether f is positive/negative and increasing/decreasing. Describe how the rate of change is changing.
dailyPhases.independentPractice: Worksheet A Problems 1-9: Graph of f with midline and five points A-E. (i) Multiple choice on f being positive/negative and increasing/decreasing on interval C to D. (ii) Describe rate of change on interval C to D. Problems 4-8: six-point graph, identify intervals. Problem 9: sketch a function that is positive and increasing, then negative and decreasing.
dailyPhases.exitEvidence: Given a piecewise function graph, identify one interval where f is positive and increasing and state the sign of the rate of change.
dailyPhases.capReflection: What courage move did you make when the rate of change sign was hard to determine?

dailyPhases.warmUp: Error analysis: 'A student says a function that is decreasing must have negative outputs. What is wrong with this reasoning?'
dailyPhases.topicIntroduction: Synthesis day: connect covariation to sign of rate of change. Review the graphical behavior table: increasing/decreasing describe direction; concave up/down describe how the rate of change itself changes. Preview connection to Topics 1.2-1.3.
dailyPhases.scaffoldedExamples: AP-style problems combining graphical and verbal descriptions. Given a piecewise function, write precise interval descriptions using input variable notation. Identify common errors: confusing 'f is increasing' with 'f is positive.'
dailyPhases.guidedPractice: Students work in pairs on mixed problems: given graph descriptions, match to correct interval notation. Given interval descriptions, sketch possible graphs.
dailyPhases.independentPractice: Mixed practice: given piecewise function graph, write interval descriptions using precise language. Problem: 'On which intervals is f negative and decreasing? On which intervals is the rate of change positive and increasing?'
dailyPhases.exitEvidence: Write two true statements about a given function graph: one about position (positive/negative) and one about direction (increasing/decreasing).
dailyPhases.capReflection: What adaptability move did you make when the graph had more features than expected?

dailyPhases.warmUp: Town population at 5-year intervals: 2000‚Üí12,400; 2005‚Üí15,800; 2010‚Üí18,600; 2015‚Üí20,200. 'How fast is the population growing per year between 2000 and 2005?'
dailyPhases.topicIntroduction: Connect 'rate of change' to 'slope.' Roller coaster analogy: going up = positive, going down = negative, at peak/valley = zero. Average rate of change formula: (f(b)‚àíf(a))/(b‚àía). Secant line connection: slope of secant = AROC. Cannot find rate of change at a point in AP Precalculus (that requires calculus).
dailyPhases.scaffoldedExamples: Example 1: Graph with points A, B, C ‚Äî determine sign of rate of change at each. Example 2: secant line concept. Example 3: compute AROC from table (population: 12,400, 15,800, 18,600, 20,200 over 5-year intervals ‚Üí 680, 560, 320 people/year ‚Äî growth slowing).
dailyPhases.guidedPractice: Students compute AROC of f(x) = x¬≤ on [1, 4]: f(1)=1, f(4)=16, AROC = 15/3 = 5. Interpret: 'The function increases by an average of 5 units per 1-unit increase in x.' Then compute AROC of g(x) = 3x‚àí7 on [2, 6].
dailyPhases.independentPractice: Find AROC of f(x) = x¬≤ + 2x on [1, 3] and interpret. Car distance: d(1)=30, d(4)=150 miles. Find AROC and explain in context. AROC of k on [2, 5] is ‚àí4; if k(2) = 10, find k(5).
dailyPhases.exitEvidence: Find the average rate of change of g(x) = 3x‚àí7 on [2, 6] and state what it means.
dailyPhases.capReflection: What persistence move did you make when the numbers looked messy?

dailyPhases.warmUp: Life expectancy data at 50-year intervals from 1800-2000. 'Over which interval is the average rate of change in life expectancy the greatest?'
dailyPhases.topicIntroduction: Compare rates of change at distinct points on a curve ‚Äî which point has a greater rate of change? Real-world positive/negative rate scenarios: money in vending machine vs items remaining, days since flu season vs people infected, Teslas on road vs years, gas remaining vs miles driven.
dailyPhases.scaffoldedExamples: Example 5: Compare rates of change at two distinct points on a curve. Example 6: Life expectancy table ‚Äî greatest AROC interval. Worksheet A: n(x) = x¬≤‚àí4, AROC over [a,b]. Worksheet B Problem 5: g(x) = 3x¬≥‚àí2x+5, compute AROC over narrowing intervals [0.5,1.5], [0.8,1.2], [0.9,1.1], [0.99,1.01] ‚Äî estimate instantaneous rate at x=1.
dailyPhases.guidedPractice: Students work through real-world scenarios: determine if two variables have positive or negative rate of change. Flu cases vs days (negative AROC), Teslas vs years (positive AROC).
dailyPhases.independentPractice: Worksheet A: AROC computations for given functions. Worksheet B Problems 1-4: real-world positive/negative rate scenarios. Problem 5: narrowing intervals for g(x) = 3x¬≥‚àí2x+5. Problem 6: graph with points A, B, C, D ‚Äî rank rates from least to greatest.
dailyPhases.exitEvidence: Graph of f with three points. Rank the rate of change at each point from least to greatest. Justify.
dailyPhases.capReflection: What courage move did you make when comparing rates of change at very close intervals?

dailyPhases.warmUp: Compute AROC of C(t) = 500(1.08)^t from t = 2 to t = 5. Interpret with units.
dailyPhases.topicIntroduction: Additional AROC practice from graphs and tables (Worksheets C-E). Ranking rates of change at multiple points on a curve. Connecting AROC to concavity preview ‚Äî if AROCs over equal intervals are increasing, the graph is concave up.
dailyPhases.scaffoldedExamples: Worksheets C-E problems: AROC calculations from graphs, tables, and equations. Comparing AROCs at multiple labeled points. Ranking from least to greatest.
dailyPhases.guidedPractice: Students solve AROC problems from multiple representations: given a graph with four points, compute and rank AROCs. Given a table, compute AROCs over different intervals and compare.
dailyPhases.independentPractice: Worksheet C-E: Rate of change calculations and comparisons from graphs and tables. Problem: given a graph of distance vs time for a car trip, describe where the car is speeding up and slowing down using AROC language.
dailyPhases.exitEvidence: A function has AROCs of 2, 4, 6, 8 over four equal-width intervals. Is the function linear, quadratic, or neither? Justify.
dailyPhases.capReflection: What adaptability move did you make when the graph was harder to read than the table?

cedTopic.title: Rates of Change in Linear and Quadratic Functions

dailyPhases.warmUp: Table: x: 1, 2, 4, 8 ‚Üí f(x): 4, 7, 10, 13. Output differences are all 3. 'Does that mean f is linear?' Check input intervals: 2‚àí1=1, 4‚àí2=2, 8‚àí4=4 ‚Äî unequal!
dailyPhases.topicIntroduction: Linear functions: constant AROC over equal-length intervals (slope is the same everywhere). Quadratic functions: AROCs change linearly ‚Äî successive differences form an arithmetic sequence. Second differences are constant. Critical: input intervals MUST be equal length.
dailyPhases.scaffoldedExamples: Example 1: f(x) = 3x+7, AROC = 3 over any interval. Example 2: g(x) = x¬≤, AROCs over [0,1],[1,2],[2,3],[3,4] = 1,3,5,7 (linear sequence, confirming quadratic). Key insight: for g(x) = x¬≤, AROCs over equal-width intervals of 2: ‚àí4, 0, 4, 8 ‚Äî each increases by 4.
dailyPhases.guidedPractice: Students compute AROCs for a new quadratic over three consecutive equal-width intervals and verify the rates form an arithmetic sequence. Then check a table with unequal intervals ‚Äî can we determine function type?
dailyPhases.independentPractice: Given tables, compute AROC over each interval. Determine if constant (linear) or changing linearly (quadratic). Compare linear vs quadratic graphs side by side. Verify that equal-length input intervals are required.
dailyPhases.exitEvidence: Given h(x) = 2x¬≤‚àíx+1, compute AROC on [0,1] and [1,2]. Are they equal? What does this tell you?
dailyPhases.capReflection: What adaptability move did you make when the pattern was not what you expected?

cedTopic.title: Rates of Change in Linear and Quadratic Functions

dailyPhases.warmUp: Concavity definitions: concave up = AROCs are increasing (graph curves up like a cup). Concave down = AROCs are decreasing (graph curves down like a frown). 'Which concavity goes with positive second differences?'
dailyPhases.topicIntroduction: Concavity connection: concave up = AROCs increasing = positive second differences. Concave down = AROCs decreasing = negative second differences. Example 3: tables with unequal intervals ‚Äî cannot determine function type even if output differences look patterned.
dailyPhases.scaffoldedExamples: Example: x: 1, 1.1, 1.2, 1.3 ‚Üí k(x): 4, 1, ‚àí1, ‚àí2. Differences: ‚àí3, ‚àí2, ‚àí1. These are increasing (from ‚àí3 to ‚àí2 to ‚àí1), so function is concave up. Example: x: 0, 2, 4, 6, 8 ‚Üí q(x): 0, 5, 8, 9, 8. Differences: 5, 3, 1, ‚àí1. Second differences: ‚àí2, ‚àí2, ‚àí2. Could be quadratic, concave down.
dailyPhases.guidedPractice: Students compute successive differences and second differences for multiple tables. Determine concavity. Verify equal-length input intervals before concluding.
dailyPhases.independentPractice: Worksheet A: Given tables, compute differences. Determine if linear, quadratic, or neither. Worksheet B: Compare linear vs quadratic graphs side by side, identify concavity.
dailyPhases.exitEvidence: x: 2, 4, 6, 8 ‚Üí f(x): 3, 7, 13, 21. Could f be quadratic? Justify using differences.
dailyPhases.capReflection: What persistence move did you make when the second differences were not exactly constant?

cedTopic.title: Rates of Change in Linear and Quadratic Functions

dailyPhases.warmUp: x: 1, 3, 5, 7, 9 ‚Üí f(x): -2, -5, -4, 1, 10. Compute successive differences. Could f be linear? Quadratic?
dailyPhases.topicIntroduction: Deeper application: connect concavity to second differences. Identify function type from AROC patterns over equal-width intervals. AP-style: 'Is the function concave up or concave down? Justify your answer.'
dailyPhases.scaffoldedExamples: Worksheet C problems: concavity analysis from tables. Problem: x: 1, 3, 5, 7 ‚Üí h(x): -1, 1, 2, 2. Determine if concave up, down, or neither. Connect to rate of change language from Topic 1.1.
dailyPhases.guidedPractice: Students work through mixed problems combining Topics 1.1-1.3: describe function behavior from a table, compute AROCs, determine concavity, justify with second differences.
dailyPhases.independentPractice: Worksheet C: Concavity analysis. Problem: given a table of a quadratic function, compute the second differences and predict the next two values. Problem: given AROCs of 2, 5, 8 over equal-width intervals, what type of function could this be?
dailyPhases.exitEvidence: A function has AROCs of ‚àí1, 2, 5, 8 over four equal-width intervals. Is it concave up or concave down? What is the second difference?
dailyPhases.capReflection: What courage move did you make when the justification required precise mathematical language?

cedTopic.title: Review: Change in Tandem, Rates of Change, Linear and Quadratic ROC

dailyPhases.warmUp: Mixed retrieval: compute AROC of f(x) = x¬≤‚àí3x on [1, 4]. Then determine if a table with constant second differences could be quadratic.
dailyPhases.topicIntroduction: Review stations covering: (1) covariation language and interval descriptions, (2) AROC computation and interpretation, (3) linear vs quadratic rate patterns and second differences.
dailyPhases.scaffoldedExamples: Station 1: piecewise function graph ‚Äî describe intervals using precise language. Station 2: compute AROC from tables and graphs. Station 3: determine function type from difference patterns.
dailyPhases.guidedPractice: Students rotate through review stations in small groups, checking answers at each station.
dailyPhases.independentPractice: Mixed AP-style problems across Topics 1.1-1.3. Cumulative review: 'On which interval is f increasing and concave down? Compute the AROC and justify.'
dailyPhases.exitEvidence: Given a graph, write one statement about direction (increasing/decreasing) and one about concavity (concave up/down).
dailyPhases.capReflection: What persistence move did you make when connecting concepts across all three topics?

cedTopic.title: Quiz: Change in Tandem, Rates of Change, Linear and Quadratic ROC

periods.notes: Quiz 1.1-1.3: covariation language, AROC computation and interpretation, linear vs quadratic rate patterns, second differences, concavity analysis.

nonInstructionArtifact.evidence: Local quiz aligned to CED competencies for Topics 1.1-1.3

cedTopic.title: Polynomial Functions and Rates of Change

dailyPhases.warmUp: f(x) = -2x‚Åµ + 3x¬≥ - x + 6. 'What is the highest power of x? What is its coefficient?' These two pieces of information control the overall shape.
dailyPhases.topicIntroduction: Polynomial definition: f(x) = a‚Çôx‚Åø + ... + a‚ÇÅx + a‚ÇÄ. Degree = highest power. Leading coefficient = a‚Çô. Leading term = a‚Çôx‚Åø. Turning points: at most n‚àí1. Relative extrema: where function switches between increasing and decreasing. Absolute extrema: global max/min. Points of inflection: where concavity changes.
dailyPhases.scaffoldedExamples: Example 1: f(x) = 3x‚Å¥ + 2x - 7 ‚Üí LC: 3, Degree: 4. y = ¬Ωx - 7x¬≥ + 11 ‚Üí rewrite: -7x¬≥ + ¬Ωx + 11 ‚Üí LC: -7, Degree: 3. Example 2: Graph of polynomial g with labeled points ‚Äî identify relative min (x=1, switches dec‚Üíinc), relative max (x=4, switches inc‚Üídec). g(1) is absolute min.
dailyPhases.guidedPractice: Students identify polynomial features from a graph: degree, leading coefficient sign, turning points, intervals of increase/decrease, extrema, inflection points.
dailyPhases.independentPractice: Identify polynomial features from a graph: degree, LC sign, turning points. Does f(x) = x‚Å¥ ‚àí 3x‚Åµ + x + 6 have an absolute min, max, or neither? Leading term is ‚àí3x‚Åµ, odd degree, negative LC ‚Äî has global max but no global min.
dailyPhases.exitEvidence: The graph of a cubic function has a local max at (‚àí1, 4) and local min at (3, ‚àí2). Describe the intervals of increase and decrease.
dailyPhases.capReflection: What persistence move did you make when the graph had more turning points than expected?

cedTopic.title: Polynomial Functions and Rates of Change

dailyPhases.warmUp: Four graphs displayed. 'Which ones could be polynomial functions? How do you know?' Polynomials are smooth and continuous ‚Äî no sharp corners, no breaks.
dailyPhases.topicIntroduction: Rate of change language on intervals: 'positive and increasing' = rising and getting steeper. 'Positive and decreasing' = rising but flattening. 'Negative and decreasing' = falling and getting steeper. 'Negative and increasing' = falling but leveling off. Example 3: on interval (3,4), graph is rising (positive ROC) but flattening (ROC decreasing).
dailyPhases.scaffoldedExamples: Example 3: describe ROC on interval (3, 4) ‚Äî positive and decreasing. Example 4: f(x) = -3x‚Å¥, leading term -3x‚Å¥, even degree negative LC ‚Üí both ends go to ‚àí‚àû.
dailyPhases.guidedPractice: Students describe rate of change on specific intervals using the four-quadrant language (positive/negative √ó increasing/decreasing).
dailyPhases.independentPractice: Worksheet A: Describe intervals of increase/decrease, identify local extrema. Worksheet B: Match polynomial equations to graphs. Sketch a polynomial with two inflection points, one absolute max, no absolute min.
dailyPhases.exitEvidence: On which interval is f increasing and the graph of f concave down? Use a given graph to identify.
dailyPhases.capReflection: What adaptability move did you make when the ROC was changing direction mid-interval?

cedTopic.title: Polynomial Functions and Rates of Change

dailyPhases.warmUp: Sketch a polynomial with one relative max, one relative minimum, and two points of inflection. Label each feature.
dailyPhases.topicIntroduction: Mixed polynomial feature analysis: combine degree, leading coefficient, extrema, inflection points, and rate of change language. Connect to concavity from Topic 1.3. AP-style: 'Describe intervals where f is increasing and concave down.'
dailyPhases.scaffoldedExamples: AP-style problems: given a polynomial graph, write a complete description ‚Äî degree (from turning points), extrema (coordinates), inflection points, intervals of increase/decrease, concavity on each interval.
dailyPhases.guidedPractice: Students work through mixed problems combining all polynomial features: identify degree, extrema, inflection, then describe ROC on specific intervals.
dailyPhases.independentPractice: Given polynomial graph, write complete description. Error analysis: 'A student says a degree 4 polynomial can have 5 turning points. Is this possible?' Problem: on h(x) on [-1,7], find open intervals where the rate of change is positive and decreasing.
dailyPhases.exitEvidence: Sketch a polynomial with degree 3, one relative max, one relative min, and no absolute extrema. Explain why no absolute extrema exist.
dailyPhases.capReflection: What courage move did you make when asked to combine multiple features into a single description?

dailyPhases.warmUp: Solve x¬≤ + 1 = 0. 'Does this equation have a solution? What kind?' Introduces the need for complex numbers.
dailyPhases.topicIntroduction: Fundamental Theorem of Algebra: degree n polynomial has exactly n zeros (counting multiplicity, including complex). Complex Conjugate Zeros Theorem: if a+bi is a zero, so is a‚àíbi. Multiplicity and graph behavior: odd multiplicity ‚Üí graph crosses x-axis (mult 1 = clean cross, mult 3+ = S-shape inflection). Even multiplicity ‚Üí graph tangent (touches and bounces back).
dailyPhases.scaffoldedExamples: Example 1: f(x) = -2x¬≥(x+1)(x‚àí4)¬≤ ‚Üí degree 6. Zeros: x=0 (mult 3, crosses with inflection), x=-1 (mult 1, crosses cleanly), x=4 (mult 2, tangent/bounces). Example 2: x=i‚àö3 is a zero ‚Üí conjugate x=‚àíi‚àö3 is also zero. Graph shows 3 real zeros, so total = 2 complex + 3 real = least degree 5.
dailyPhases.guidedPractice: Students find zeros of g(x) = x¬≥‚àí4x = x(x‚àí2)(x+2). All multiplicity 1 (odd), so graph crosses at every zero. State degree, number of zeros, and behavior at each.
dailyPhases.independentPractice: g(x) = -2x(x‚àí5)¬≥(x+1)¬≤. Find all zeros with multiplicity and describe graph behavior at each. A polynomial has zeros x=3 (mult 2), x=3i, x=4‚àíi. What is the least possible degree?
dailyPhases.exitEvidence: Find all zeros of f(x) = x¬≥‚àí4x with multiplicity. State whether graph crosses or is tangent at each zero.
dailyPhases.capReflection: What courage move did you make when the numbers involved imaginary units?

dailyPhases.warmUp: Given that 2+i is a zero of f(x) = x¬≥‚àí6x¬≤+13x‚àí10, what must also be a zero? Why?
dailyPhases.topicIntroduction: Complex zeros: given one complex zero, find all zeros using conjugate pairs and synthetic division. Form quadratic factor from conjugate pair: (x‚àí(2+i))(x‚àí(2‚àíi)) = x¬≤‚àí4x+5. Then divide the polynomial by this quadratic to find remaining real zeros. Calculator verification: graph shows only real zeros; complex zeros do not appear on the graph.
dailyPhases.scaffoldedExamples: Example: 2+i is zero of x¬≥‚àí6x¬≤+13x‚àí10. Conjugate 2‚àíi is also zero. Quadratic factor: x¬≤‚àí4x+5. Divide: (x¬≥‚àí6x¬≤+13x‚àí10) √∑ (x¬≤‚àí4x+5) = x‚àí2. All zeros: 2+i, 2‚àíi, 2.
dailyPhases.guidedPractice: Students factor a polynomial completely given one complex zero. Use conjugate pair to form quadratic factor, then divide to find remaining zeros.
dailyPhases.independentPractice: Worksheet A-C: Finding zeros given one complex zero, verifying with calculator. Factor polynomials completely using known complex zeros. Problem: given that 3-2i is a zero of p(x) = x‚Å¥‚àí6x¬≥+14x¬≤‚àí6x+13, find all zeros.
dailyPhases.exitEvidence: Given that 1+i is a zero of f(x) = x¬≥‚àí3x¬≤+4x‚àí2, find all zeros.
dailyPhases.capReflection: What persistence move did you make when the synthetic division had complex intermediate steps?

dailyPhases.warmUp: Solve (x‚àí3)(x+1)(x+4) > 0. 'Where do we start?' Find the zeros first.
dailyPhases.topicIntroduction: Sign chart method (4 steps): (1) solve f(x)=0 to find all real zeros, (2) place zeros on number line creating intervals, (3) test a value in each interval to determine sign, (4) write solution in interval notation. Rational Root Theorem: possible rational zeros = factors of constant / factors of leading coefficient.
dailyPhases.scaffoldedExamples: Example 3: solve (x‚àí3)(x+1)(x+4) > 0. Roots: x=-4, -1, 3. Test intervals: (‚àí‚àû,‚àí4): test x=-5 ‚Üí (‚àí)(‚àí)(‚àí) = negative. (‚àí4,‚àí1): test x=-2 ‚Üí (‚àí)(+)(‚àí) = positive. (‚àí1,3): test x=0 ‚Üí (‚àí)(+)(+) = negative. (3,‚àû): test x=4 ‚Üí (+)(+)(+) = positive. Solution: (‚àí4,‚àí1) ‚à™ (3,‚àû).
dailyPhases.guidedPractice: Students solve (x+2)¬≤(x‚àí5) ‚â§ 0 using sign chart. Root at x=-2 (even ‚Äî tangent) and x=5 (odd ‚Äî crosses). Include endpoints where f(x)=0.
dailyPhases.independentPractice: Worksheet D-E: Sign chart problems. Solve polynomial inequalities. Apply Rational Root Theorem: list all possible rational zeros of f(x) = 2x¬≥‚àí3x¬≤+5x‚àí6. Solve (x+3)(x‚àí1)(x‚àí2) < 0.
dailyPhases.exitEvidence: Solve (x+4)/(x‚àí2) ‚â§ 0 (preview of rational inequalities). Show sign chart with critical values.
dailyPhases.capReflection: What adaptability move did you make when the inequality was ‚â§ instead of <?

dailyPhases.warmUp: As x gets very large (x=1000, x=1,000,000), what happens to f(x) = 3x¬≤‚àí5? What about g(x) = -x¬≥+100x?
dailyPhases.topicIntroduction: End behavior determined entirely by leading term a‚Çôx‚Åø. Step 1 ‚Äî RIGHT side (x‚Üí‚àû): positive LC ‚Üí up (lim = ‚àû), negative LC ‚Üí down (lim = ‚àí‚àû). Step 2 ‚Äî LEFT side (x‚Üí‚àí‚àû): even degree ‚Üí same direction as right, odd degree ‚Üí opposite. Four patterns table: even+/even‚àí/odd+/odd‚àí.
dailyPhases.scaffoldedExamples: Example 1a: f(x) = 4x‚Åµ, odd degree, positive LC ‚Üí right up, left down. lim(x‚Üí‚àû) = ‚àû, lim(x‚Üí‚àí‚àû) = ‚àí‚àû. Example 1b: g(x) = ¬Ωx‚Å¥, even, positive ‚Üí both up. Example 1c: y = -2(x+3)‚Å∂, leading term -2x‚Å∂, even, negative ‚Üí both down. Example 3: f(x) = -3x‚Å¥+x‚àí7, even, negative ‚Üí lim(x‚Üí‚àí‚àû) = ‚àí‚àû, lim(x‚Üí‚àû) = ‚àí‚àû.
dailyPhases.guidedPractice: Students write limit statements for g(x) = -2x‚Å¥+x‚àí3 (even, negative ‚Üí both ends down). Sketch a polynomial where lim(x‚Üí‚àí‚àû) = ‚àí‚àû and lim(x‚Üí‚àû) = ‚àû (odd, positive LC).
dailyPhases.independentPractice: Describe end behavior and write limit statements for f(x) = -4x¬≥+2x‚àí1. Find leading term of k(x) = 8x¬≤+4‚àíx‚Åµ (‚àíx‚Åµ, odd, negative). Match four graphs to four equations based on end behavior.
dailyPhases.exitEvidence: Without graphing, describe end behavior of g(x) = 4x‚Å∂‚àí2x¬≥+1. Write limit statements.
dailyPhases.capReflection: What persistence move did you make when the leading coefficient was negative?

dailyPhases.warmUp: h(x) = 3‚àíx‚Åµ. 'The leading term is not obvious. Rewrite in standard form.' 3‚àíx‚Åµ = -x‚Åµ+3. Leading term: -x‚Åµ.
dailyPhases.topicIntroduction: Hidden leading terms: rewrite in standard form first. Factored form: multiply leading terms to find the overall leading term. m(x) = 2x(x‚àí1)(6‚àíx) ‚Üí 2x¬∑x¬∑(‚àíx) = -2x¬≥. Match four polynomial functions to graphs based on end behavior analysis alone.
dailyPhases.scaffoldedExamples: Example 2a: h(x) = 3‚àíx‚Åµ = -x‚Åµ+3 ‚Üí odd, negative ‚Üí right down, left up. Example 2b: k(x) = 8x¬≤+4‚àíx‚Åµ ‚Üí -x‚Åµ ‚Üí odd, negative. Example 2c: m(x) = 2x(x‚àí1)(6‚àíx) ‚Üí -2x¬≥ ‚Üí odd, negative. Match four polynomials to four graphs.
dailyPhases.guidedPractice: Students identify leading terms from factored form and match to graphs. Write limit statements for each.
dailyPhases.independentPractice: Worksheet A: Describe end behavior of given polynomials (some in standard form, some in factored form). Worksheet B: Match polynomials to graphs using end behavior. Write limit statements for each.
dailyPhases.exitEvidence: Write limit statements for f(x) = -2x‚Å¥+x‚àí3.
dailyPhases.capReflection: What adaptability move did you make when the polynomial was given in factored form?

cedTopic.title: Review: Polynomial and Rational Functions Part 1

dailyPhases.warmUp: Cumulative retrieval: describe end behavior of f(x) = -3x‚Åµ+2x¬≥‚àíx+1. Find zeros of g(x) = x(x‚àí2)¬≤(x+3) with multiplicity.
dailyPhases.topicIntroduction: Passwater review stations A-D covering all polynomial topics: (A) covariation and rate of change, (B) linear/quadratic rate patterns, (C) polynomial features and complex zeros, (D) end behavior and limit statements.

dailyPhases.guidedPractice: Students rotate through review stations. Check answers at each station before moving on.

dailyPhases.warmUp: f(x) = (3x¬≤+4)/(x¬≤‚àí1). 'As x gets very large, both numerator and denominator grow like x¬≤. What does the fraction approach?' Preview: compare degrees.
dailyPhases.topicIntroduction: Rational functions: p(x)/q(x). End behavior depends on comparing n (numerator degree) and d (denominator degree). Case I: n=d ‚Üí HA at y=a/b (ratio of leading coefficients). Case II: n<d ‚Üí HA at y=0 (denominator dominates). Case III: n>d ‚Üí no HA; if n=d+1 exactly, slant asymptote exists.
dailyPhases.scaffoldedExamples: Example 1a: f(x) = (3x¬≤+4x‚àí7)/(5x¬≤‚àí3) ‚Üí n=2, d=2 ‚Üí Case I ‚Üí HA y=3/5. Example 1b: y = (2x‚àí5)/(x¬≤+3x+2) ‚Üí n=1, d=2 ‚Üí Case II ‚Üí HA y=0. Example 1c: g(x) = (2x¬≤‚àí4)/(5x+9) ‚Üí n=2, d=1 ‚Üí Case III, slant (n=d+1). Example 1d: k(x) = 3/(x¬≤+3x‚àí7) ‚Üí n=0, d=2 ‚Üí Case II ‚Üí HA y=0.
dailyPhases.guidedPractice: Students find horizontal asymptotes for three rational functions by comparing degrees and leading coefficients. Classify which case applies.
dailyPhases.independentPractice: Find HA of y = (3x¬≤‚àí1)/(2x¬≤+5x+7). Find HA of h(x) = (4x¬≤‚àí5x‚àí2)/(x¬≥+6x). Does y = (3x¬≤+1)/(x‚àí1) have a horizontal or slant asymptote? Explain.
dailyPhases.exitEvidence: Find the horizontal asymptote of h(x) = (7x¬≤‚àí3)/(2x¬≤+5x‚àí1) or state none exists.
dailyPhases.capReflection: What persistence move did you make when the degrees were the same?

dailyPhases.warmUp: Write limit statements for f(x) = (2x¬≥+4x‚àí1)/(6x¬≥‚àíx¬≤+4). n=3, d=3 ‚Üí HA y=2/6=1/3. Both limits approach 1/3.
dailyPhases.topicIntroduction: Limit statements for rational functions: connect HA to lim(x‚Üí‚àû) and lim(x‚Üí‚àí‚àû). Slant asymptote when n=d+1: perform polynomial long division, quotient is the slant asymptote. Example 3: which functions have a slant asymptote parallel to y=¬Ωx? Only those where n=d+1 and leading coefficient ratio = 1/2.
dailyPhases.scaffoldedExamples: Example 2: Write limit statements for g(x) = (5x¬≤‚àí8x+9)/(2x¬≥+x‚àí1) ‚Üí HA y=0, both limits = 0. h(x) = (‚àí3x‚Å¥‚àíx¬≤+x)/(x¬≥+4x+4) ‚Üí n=4, d=3, n>d+1 ‚Üí leading ratio is ‚àí3x, as x‚Üí‚àû, h(x)‚Üí‚àí‚àû.
dailyPhases.guidedPractice: Students write limit statements for three rational functions, identifying HA or slant asymptote for each.
dailyPhases.independentPractice: Worksheet A: Find HA, classify end behavior. Worksheet B: Find slant asymptotes for n=d+1 cases. Write limit statements for each.
dailyPhases.exitEvidence: Does f(x) = (5x¬≤+3)/(2x¬≤‚àí1) have a horizontal or slant asymptote? Write equation and limit statements.
dailyPhases.capReflection: What adaptability move did you make when the function had no horizontal asymptote?

dailyPhases.warmUp: Error analysis: 'A student says f(x) = (2x+1)/(x‚àí3) has HA y=0 because the numerator has lower degree.' Is this correct? n=1, d=1 ‚Üí same degree ‚Üí HA y=2/1=2, not 0.
dailyPhases.topicIntroduction: Mixed rational end behavior: combine all three cases. Compare HA across multiple functions. AP-style: 'Write limit statements and identify the horizontal asymptote.' Common mistake: trying to divide every term instead of just comparing leading terms.
dailyPhases.scaffoldedExamples: Mixed problems: classify end behavior, find HAs and slant asymptotes, write limit statements. Compare: f(x) = (3x¬≤+1)/(2x¬≤‚àí5) has HA y=3/2; g(x) = (3x¬≤+1)/(2x¬≥‚àí5) has HA y=0.
dailyPhases.guidedPractice: Students work through error analysis problems: identify mistakes in HA calculations, correct them, and explain.
dailyPhases.independentPractice: Mixed practice: classify end behavior of five rational functions. Write limit statements. Problem: rank three rational functions by their HA values.
dailyPhases.exitEvidence: Given f(x) = (ax¬≤+b)/(cx¬≤+d), for what values of a and c would the HA be y=3?
dailyPhases.capReflection: What courage move did you make when two different functions had the same HA?

dailyPhases.warmUp: f(x) = (x‚àí2)/((x+6)(x‚àí3)). 'Before doing any algebra: where is f(x) = 0? Where is it undefined?' Zeros from numerator, undefined from denominator.
dailyPhases.topicIntroduction: Rational function zeros: numerator = 0 AND denominator ‚âÝ 0. If a zero of numerator also makes denominator zero, it is NOT a zero of f. Passwater's 7-step method for rational inequalities: (1) get 0 on one side, (2) write as single fraction, (3) set numerator and denominator to 0 for critical values, (4) create sign chart, (5) mark denominator zeros as never included, (6) test each interval, (7) write solution.
dailyPhases.scaffoldedExamples: Example 1: solve (x‚àí2)/((x+6)(x‚àí3)) ‚â• 0. Numerator zero: x=2. Denominator zeros: x=-6, x=3. Sign chart with critical values -6, 2, 3. Test intervals. Include x=2 (‚â•0). Exclude x=-6 and x=3 (undefined). Solution: (‚àí‚àû,‚àí6) ‚à™ [2,3).
dailyPhases.guidedPractice: Students solve the same inequality step-by-step, building the sign chart together. Then try: find zeros of f(x) = (x¬≤‚àí9)/(x¬≤‚àí4x+3). Numerator zeros: ¬±3. Denominator zeros: 1, 3. x=3 makes both zero ‚Üí not a zero of f. Only zero: x=-3.
dailyPhases.independentPractice: Find zeros with domain restrictions. Solve (2x)/(x+1) < 0. Identify all zeros and undefined values, build sign chart.
dailyPhases.exitEvidence: Find all zeros of r(x) = (x¬≤‚àí5x+6)/(x¬≤‚àí4) and state the domain.
dailyPhases.capReflection: What persistence move did you make when the numerator and denominator shared a zero?

dailyPhases.warmUp: Example 2: solve (x¬≤‚àí4)/(x¬≤‚àí10x+25) < 0. Factor: (x‚àí2)(x+2)/(x‚àí5)¬≤. Numerator zeros: -2, 2. Denominator zero: 5 (excluded).
dailyPhases.topicIntroduction: Factoring both numerator and denominator for rational inequality solving. Domain restrictions: exclude denominator zeros from solution sets. EK 1.8.A.3: denominator zeros are NEVER included in solution sets.
dailyPhases.scaffoldedExamples: Example 2 continued: sign chart with critical values -2, 2, 5. Test: (‚àí‚àû,‚àí2) negative, (‚àí2,2) positive, (2,5) negative, (5,‚àû) negative. Solution: (‚àí2,2). Find zeros of f(x) = (x¬≤‚àí9)/(x¬≤‚àí2x‚àí15) = (x‚àí3)(x+3)/((x‚àí5)(x+3)). Cancel (x+3): simplified is (x‚àí3)/(x‚àí5). Zero at x=3 (valid, not a denominator zero).
dailyPhases.guidedPractice: Students find zeros of f(x) = (x¬≤‚àí9)/(x¬≤‚àí2x‚àí15), factor both, check for canceling factors.
dailyPhases.independentPractice: Worksheet A: Find zeros with domain restrictions. Problem: Find zeros of f(x) = (x¬≤‚àí9)/(x¬≤‚àí2x‚àí15). State domain. Watch for factors that cancel.
dailyPhases.exitEvidence: Find the zeros of y = (3x¬≤+x‚àí2)/((x‚àí1)(x+5)). Factor numerator: (3x‚àí2)(x+1). Check domain restrictions.
dailyPhases.capReflection: What adaptability move did you make when a factor appeared in both numerator and denominator?

dailyPhases.warmUp: Solve ((x‚àí1)(x+2)¬≤)/(x‚àí2) ‚â• 0. Numerator zeros: x=1, x=-2 (even multiplicity). Denominator zero: x=2 (never included).
dailyPhases.topicIntroduction: Even multiplicity at critical values: if a factor appears to an even power, the sign does NOT change across that critical value. Connecting zeros to domain restrictions in rational inequalities.
dailyPhases.scaffoldedExamples: Problem: Solve ((x‚àí1)(x+2)¬≤)/(x‚àí2) ‚â• 0. At x=-2, even multiplicity means the expression equals 0 (included in ‚â•0). Sign chart: critical values -2, 1, 2. At x=-2, sign doesn't change. Solution includes x=-2 and x=1 (both zeros).
dailyPhases.guidedPractice: Students work through problems with even multiplicity. Solve 1/(x‚àí1)¬≤ ‚â§ 0 ‚Äî is this possible? (x‚àí1)¬≤ is always positive, so 1/(x‚àí1)¬≤ is always positive. Solution: empty set.
dailyPhases.independentPractice: Worksheet B: Solve rational inequalities using sign charts. Even multiplicity problems. Solve (x+4)/(x‚àí2) ‚â§ 0 with sign chart. Explain why each endpoint is included or excluded.
dailyPhases.exitEvidence: Solve (x+4)/(x‚àí2) ‚â§ 0. Show sign chart with all critical values marked.
dailyPhases.capReflection: What courage move did you make when the solution set was empty?

dailyPhases.warmUp: Graph f(x) = 1/x on calculator. 'What happens as x approaches 0 from the left? From the right?' The graph shoots toward infinity ‚Äî vertical asymptote. Now consider g(x) = (x¬≤‚àí4)/(x‚àí2). What happens at x=2? Is it the same?
dailyPhases.topicIntroduction: Vertical asymptote: denominator zero where factor does NOT cancel with numerator. 4-step process: (1) factor both numerator and denominator, (2) cancel common factors, (3) remaining denominator zeros are VAs, (4) write one-sided limit statements.
dailyPhases.scaffoldedExamples: Example 1: f(x) = (x‚àí2)(x+3)/((x+3)(x‚àí5)). Cancel (x+3): simplified is (x‚àí2)/(x‚àí5). VA at x=5 (remaining denominator zero). The canceled factor (x+3) produces a hole at x=-3 (Topic 1.10). Example 3: g(x) = (x‚àí1)(x+2)/(x‚àí1)¬≤. Cancel one (x‚àí1): simplified is (x+2)/(x‚àí1). One (x‚àí1) remains ‚Üí VA at x=1.
dailyPhases.guidedPractice: Students find VAs of p(x) = (x¬≤‚àí1)/(x+1)¬≤. Factor: (x‚àí1)(x+1)/(x+1)¬≤. Cancel one (x+1): simplified is (x‚àí1)/(x+1). VA at x=-1.
dailyPhases.independentPractice: Find all VAs of f(x) = (x‚àí1)(x‚àí5)/((x‚àí5)(x+2)). State one-sided limit statements at each VA. Find all VAs of f(x) = (2x+6)/(x‚àí3) and write lim(x‚Üí3‚Åª) and lim(x‚Üí3‚Å∫).
dailyPhases.exitEvidence: Find vertical asymptotes of k(x) = (x¬≤+1)/(x¬≤‚àíx‚àí6) and describe behavior near each.
dailyPhases.capReflection: What persistence move did you make when the factorization was tricky?

dailyPhases.warmUp: Write one-sided limit statements for g(x) = (x‚àí2)(x+4)/((x‚àí2)(x‚àí3)). First cancel (x‚àí2). VA at x=3. Test values on either side.
dailyPhases.topicIntroduction: One-sided limit statements at each VA: test values on either side to determine ¬±‚àû direction. Example 2: at x=2, test x=1.9 ‚Üí numerator (0.9)(4.9)>0, denominator -0.1<0 ‚Üí f(1.9)<0. Test x=2.1 ‚Üí numerator (1.1)(5.1)>0, denominator 0.1>0 ‚Üí f(2.1)>0. Therefore: lim(x‚Üí2‚Åª) = ‚àí‚àû, lim(x‚Üí2‚Å∫) = +‚àû.
dailyPhases.scaffoldedExamples: Example 2: Write one-sided limits for f(x) = (x‚àí1)(x+3)/(x‚àí2). VA at x=2. Test values: f(1.9)<0, f(2.1)>0. lim(x‚Üí2‚Åª) = ‚àí‚àû, lim(x‚Üí2‚Å∫) = +‚àû. Example 3: partial cancellation still leaves VA.
dailyPhases.guidedPractice: Students write one-sided limits for g(x) = (x¬≤‚àí9)/(x¬≤‚àíx‚àí6) = (x‚àí3)(x+3)/((x‚àí3)(x+1)). Cancel (x‚àí3): VA at x=-1. Test values near x=-1.
dailyPhases.independentPractice: Worksheet A-B: VA identification and limit statements. Write one-sided limits at each VA. Problem: write equation of rational function with VA at x=1 where lim(x‚Üí1‚Åª)=‚àí‚àû and lim(x‚Üí1‚Å∫)=+‚àû, and a hole at x=3.
dailyPhases.exitEvidence: Find all VAs of g(x) = (x¬≤‚àí9)/(x¬≤‚àíx‚àí6). Write one-sided limit statements at each.
dailyPhases.capReflection: What courage move did you make when testing values near the asymptote?

dailyPhases.warmUp: Find all VAs of h(x) = 1/(x¬≥+4x). Factor denominator: x(x¬≤+4). Which factor produces a real zero? x=0 only (x¬≤+4=0 has complex solutions).
dailyPhases.topicIntroduction: Worksheet C: Solving rational inequalities using VA analysis. Connecting VAs to sign charts ‚Äî VAs create the boundaries for sign chart intervals. Construct a rational function with specified VA behavior.
dailyPhases.scaffoldedExamples: Rational inequality using VA analysis: solve 1/(x‚àí3) > 0. VA at x=3. Sign chart: for x<3, 1/(negative) = negative. For x>3, 1/(positive) = positive. Solution: (3,‚àû). Construct: rational function with VA at x=1 where lim(x‚Üí1‚Åª)=‚àí‚àû, lim(x‚Üí1‚Å∫)=+‚àû, and a hole at x=3.
dailyPhases.guidedPractice: Students solve rational inequalities using VA analysis. Connect to sign chart method from Topic 1.5/1.8.
dailyPhases.independentPractice: Worksheet C: Rational inequality problems using VA analysis. Construct a rational function with specified VA behavior and a hole at x=3.
dailyPhases.exitEvidence: Construct a rational function with VA at x=-2 (both sides ‚Üí +‚àû) and a zero at x=1.
dailyPhases.capReflection: What adaptability move did you make when constructing the function?

dailyPhases.warmUp: f(x) = (x¬≤‚àí4)/(x‚àí2). Factor numerator: (x‚àí2)(x+2)/(x‚àí2). After canceling, simplified form is x+2. But is f(2) defined? Calculator gives error. Simplified form gives 4. This 'missing point' is a hole.
dailyPhases.topicIntroduction: Holes (removable discontinuities): factor cancels completely in numerator and denominator. x-coordinate = value making canceled factor zero. y-coordinate = evaluate simplified form at that value. Holes vs VAs comparison table: hole = factor cancels, both sides equal (finite). VA = factor does not cancel, sides diverge to ¬±‚àû.
dailyPhases.scaffoldedExamples: Example 1: f(x) = (x‚àí2)(x+3)/((x+3)(x‚àí5)). Cancel (x+3): simplified is (x‚àí2)/(x‚àí5). x=-3: factor canceled ‚Üí hole. y-coordinate: (-3‚àí2)/(-3‚àí5) = -5/-8 = 5/8. Hole at (-3, 5/8). x=5: factor remains ‚Üí VA at x=5.
dailyPhases.guidedPractice: Students classify denominator zeros for g(x) = (x¬≤‚àí5x+6)/(x‚àí2) = (x‚àí2)(x‚àí3)/(x‚àí2). Cancel (x‚àí2): simplified is x‚àí3. Hole at x=2, y=2‚àí3=-1. Hole at (2,-1). No VA.
dailyPhases.independentPractice: Worksheet A: Identify holes and calculate coordinates. Worksheet B: Rewrite in simplified form, identify all discontinuities. Find all holes and VAs of f(x) = (x‚àí1)(x‚àí5)/((x‚àí5)(x+2)).
dailyPhases.exitEvidence: Find the hole in g(x) = (x¬≥‚àí8)/(x‚àí2). Factor: (x‚àí2)(x¬≤+2x+4)/(x‚àí2). Hole at x=2, y=4+4+4=12.
dailyPhases.capReflection: What courage move did you make when you realized a 'disappearing' factor means a hole, not an asymptote?

dailyPhases.warmUp: Classify: g(x) = (x‚àí2)(x+4)/((x‚àí2)(x‚àí3)). Cancel (x‚àí2): simplified is (x+4)/(x‚àí3). Since (x‚àí2) canceled completely, both limits at x=2 are equal. Evaluate simplified at x=2: 6/(-1)=-6. Hole at (2,-6). VA at x=3.
dailyPhases.topicIntroduction: Distinguishing holes from VAs: always factor completely first. Partial powers: (x‚àí2)¬≤ in numerator, (x‚àí1) in denominator. Cancel one (x‚àí2): simplified is (x‚àí2)/(x‚àí1). One (x‚àí1) remains ‚Üí VA at x=-1. (x‚àí2) fully canceled ‚Üí hole at x=2, y=0.
dailyPhases.scaffoldedExamples: Example 2: y = (x+1)(x‚àí2)¬≤/((x‚àí2)(x+1)¬≤). Cancel one (x‚àí2) and one (x+1): simplified is (x‚àí2)/(x+1). x=2: fully canceled ‚Üí hole at (2,0). x=-1: (x+1) remains ‚Üí VA at x=-1. Example 3: construct function with hole at x=3, VAs at x=1 and x=-4: f(x) = (x‚àí3)/((x‚àí3)(x‚àí1)(x+4)).
dailyPhases.guidedPractice: Students distinguish holes from VAs in three rational functions. Construct a rational function with hole at x=1 and VA at x=4 where lim(x‚Üí4‚Åª)=+‚àû, lim(x‚Üí4‚Å∫)=‚àí‚àû.
dailyPhases.independentPractice: Worksheet A-B: Identify holes and calculate coordinates. Rewrite in simplified form. Construct rational function with holes at x=2 and x=5, VA at x=0, zero at x=1.
dailyPhases.exitEvidence: Classify: f(x) = (x‚àí3)(x+1)/(x‚àí3) has a ___ at x=___. g(x) = (x+2)/(x‚àí3) has a ___ at x=___.
dailyPhases.capReflection: What adaptability move did you make when a factor appeared with different powers in numerator and denominator?

dailyPhases.warmUp: Find all zeros, holes, and VAs of f(x) = (x‚àí1)(x‚àí5)/((x‚àí5)(x+2)). Factor, cancel, classify each feature.
dailyPhases.topicIntroduction: Mixed practice: classify all discontinuities of rational functions (holes, VAs, domain). Connect to Topic 1.8 (zeros where numerator = 0 and denominator ‚âÝ 0) and Topic 1.9 (VAs where denominator = 0 and factor doesn't cancel). Complete rational function analysis: zeros, holes, VAs, domain, end behavior.
dailyPhases.scaffoldedExamples: Complete analysis of f(x) = (x‚àí1)(x‚àí5)/((x‚àí5)(x+2)). Cancel (x‚àí5): simplified is (x‚àí1)/(x+2). Zeros: x=1 (numerator zero, not a denominator zero). Holes: x=5 (canceled), y=(5‚àí1)/(5+2)=4/7. VAs: x=-2. Domain: all reals except x=5 and x=-2.
dailyPhases.guidedPractice: Students work through complete rational function analysis: given equation, list all features in order. Construct function with zeros at x=1, hole at x=2, VA at x=0.
dailyPhases.independentPractice: Mixed problems: find all zeros, holes, VAs of given rational functions. Construct rational function with specified features. How many VAs and holes does f(x) = (x¬≤‚àí6x+3)/((x¬≤‚àí2)(x¬≤‚àí3x‚àí5)¬≤) have?
dailyPhases.exitEvidence: Write a rational function that has zeros at x=-1 and x=3, a hole at x=2, and a VA at x=4.
dailyPhases.capReflection: What persistence move did you make when tracking all the features at once?

cedTopic.title: Equivalent Representations of Polynomial and Rational Expressions

dailyPhases.warmUp: f(x) = (x¬≥‚àíx)/(x¬≤‚àí1). Write in three forms: factored, expanded, simplified. 'What does each form reveal?'
dailyPhases.topicIntroduction: Equivalent representations: factored form ‚Üí zeros, holes, VAs. Expanded form ‚Üí coefficients, degree. Graph ‚Üí visual features. Long division for slant asymptotes: f(x)/g(x) = q(x) + r(x)/g(x). Binomial Theorem: (a+b)‚Åø with Pascal's Triangle coefficients.
dailyPhases.scaffoldedExamples: Example 1: h(x) = (x¬≤‚àí4)/(x¬≤+7x+10) = (x‚àí2)(x+2)/((x+2)(x+5)). Cancel (x+2): hole at x=-2, y=-4/3. VA at x=-5. Zero at x=2. Example 2: Long division of (x¬≤+6x+5)/(2x+1). Quotient: x/2+11/4, remainder 9/4. Slant asymptote: y=x/2+11/4. Example 3: Expand (x+2)‚Åµ using Pascal's Triangle row 5: 1,5,10,10,5,1 ‚Üí x‚Åµ+10x‚Å¥+40x¬≥+80x¬≤+80x+32.
dailyPhases.guidedPractice: Students use long division to find slant asymptote of f(x) = (x¬≤‚àí6x+7)/(x‚àí1). Divide and identify quotient as slant asymptote.
dailyPhases.independentPractice: Convert between forms. Use long division for slant asymptote of r(x) = (2x¬≤+4x+7)/(6x‚àí5). Expand (x+5)‚Å∂: what is the coefficient of x‚Å¥? Use C(6,2)¬∑5¬≤ = 15¬∑25 = 375.
dailyPhases.exitEvidence: Use long division to find slant asymptote of f(x) = (2x¬≤+3x‚àí1)/(x+4).
dailyPhases.capReflection: What persistence move did you make when the long division produced fractions?

cedTopic.title: Equivalent Representations of Polynomial and Rational Expressions

dailyPhases.warmUp: Analyze d(x) = (2x¬≤+4x+1)/(2x‚àí1). Does it have a horizontal or slant asymptote? n=2, d=1 ‚Üí slant (n=d+1). How many VAs? How many holes?
dailyPhases.topicIntroduction: Convert between representations and reconstruct equation from graph features. Problem: given graph with zero at x=-1, hole at x=3, VA at x=2, HA at y=1, write a possible equation. Must include (x+1) in numerator for zero, (x‚àí3) in both for hole, (x‚àí2) in denominator for VA, and equal degrees with leading coefficient ratio 1 for HA.
dailyPhases.scaffoldedExamples: Given graph features: zero at x=-1 ‚Üí factor (x+1) in numerator only. Hole at x=3 ‚Üí factor (x‚àí3) in both. VA at x=2 ‚Üí factor (x‚àí2) in denominator only. HA y=1 ‚Üí equal degrees, leading coeff ratio 1. Possible: f(x) = (x+1)(x‚àí3)/((x‚àí3)(x‚àí2)).
dailyPhases.guidedPractice: Students reconstruct equations from graph features. Convert between factored and expanded forms.
dailyPhases.independentPractice: Worksheet A-B: Convert forms, identify features from each representation. Problem: f(x) = (x¬≤+x‚àí2)/(x¬≤‚àí2x‚àí3), provide complete analysis ‚Äî factored form, zeros, holes, VAs, HAs, domain, sketch.
dailyPhases.exitEvidence: Given a graph of a rational function with a zero at x=3 and a hole at x=-1, write a possible factored form.
dailyPhases.capReflection: What adaptability move did you make when multiple equations could match the same graph?

cedTopic.title: Equivalent Representations of Polynomial and Rational Expressions

dailyPhases.warmUp: f(x) = (x¬≤+x‚àí2)/(x¬≤‚àí2x‚àí3). Factor completely: (x+2)(x‚àí1)/((x‚àí3)(x+1)). No common factors. List all features.
dailyPhases.topicIntroduction: Worksheet C: comprehensive equivalent representation problems. Connect to all previous rational function topics. AP-style: 'Given the graph, write a possible equation in factored form.' Full analysis: factored form, expanded form, zeros, holes, VAs, HAs, slant asymptote, domain, sketch.
dailyPhases.scaffoldedExamples: Complete analysis of f(x) = (x¬≤+x‚àí2)/(x¬≤‚àí2x‚àí3) = (x+2)(x‚àí1)/((x‚àí3)(x+1)). Zeros: x=-2, x=1. No holes (no common factors). VAs: x=3, x=-1. HA: n=d=2 ‚Üí y=1/1=1. Domain: all reals except x=3 and x=-1.
dailyPhases.guidedPractice: Students provide complete analysis for two rational functions: identify all features from the equation, then sketch the graph.
dailyPhases.independentPractice: Worksheet C: Full analysis ‚Äî factored form, expanded form, zeros, holes, VAs, HAs, slant asymptote, domain, sketch. Multiple functions requiring long division and equivalent form conversion.
dailyPhases.exitEvidence: Given a rational function equation, list all features: zeros, holes, VAs, HA or slant, domain.
dailyPhases.capReflection: What courage move did you make when the complete analysis required tracking many features?

dailyPhases.warmUp: Cumulative retrieval: find all features of f(x) = (x¬≤‚àí4)/(x¬≤+7x+10). Zeros, holes, VAs, HA.

dailyPhases.guidedPractice: Students rotate through review stations, checking answers at each.
dailyPhases.independentPractice: Mixed AP-style problems across Topics 1.7-1.11. FRQ 1 and FRQ 2 task model practice.
dailyPhases.exitEvidence: Write a complete description of a rational function given its graph.

dailyPhases.warmUp: f(3) = 5. What is g(3) when g(x) = 2f(x)‚àí1? g(3) = 2(5)‚àí1 = 9. What about g(3) when g(x) = f(x+2)? g(3) = f(5).
dailyPhases.topicIntroduction: Transformations: vertical translation f(x)+k (up k), horizontal translation f(x+h) (left h), vertical dilation a¬∑f(x) (stretch by |a|), horizontal dilation f(bx) (compress by |1/b|), reflections (a<0 ‚Üí x-axis, b<0 ‚Üí y-axis). General form: a¬∑f(b(x‚àíh))+k. Transformation order: horizontal first (translate, then dilate), then vertical (dilate, then translate).
dailyPhases.scaffoldedExamples: Describe transformations from f(x) = x¬≥ to g(x) = -2(x+1)¬≥‚àí4. Rewrite: g(x) = -2f(x+1)‚àí4. Horizontal shift left 1, vertical stretch by 2, reflect over x-axis, vertical shift down 4.
dailyPhases.guidedPractice: Students describe transformations step by step: f(x) = x¬≤ ‚Üí g(x) = 3(x‚àí2)¬≤+1. Shift right 2, stretch vertically by 3, shift up 1.
dailyPhases.independentPractice: Describe all transformations from f(x) = 1/x to g(x) = -3/(x‚àí2)+1. Describe transformations for five different parent functions.
dailyPhases.exitEvidence: Describe all transformations from f(x) = ‚àöx to g(x) = -¬Ω‚àö(x+3)‚àí2.
dailyPhases.capReflection: What persistence move did you make when the order of transformations mattered?

dailyPhases.warmUp: f has domain [-6, 9] and range [-8, 10), increasing on [-6, 1] and [5, 9), decreasing on [1, 5]. Find domain and range of q(x) = 4‚àí2n(5‚àíx).
dailyPhases.topicIntroduction: Domain and range under transformations. Point mapping: (p,q) on f maps to (p/b‚àíc/b, aq+d) on g. Example 1: g(x) = 2f(x‚àí1)+3 with f domain [-4,6] and range [-2,5]. Domain of g: x‚àí1‚àà[-4,6] ‚Üí x‚àà[-3,7]. Range: 2¬∑[-2,5]+3 = [-1,13]. Point mapping: (4,2) on f ‚Üí (5,7) on g.
dailyPhases.scaffoldedExamples: Example 1: g(x) = 2f(x‚àí1)+3. Domain [-3,7], range [-1,13]. g(0) = 2f(‚àí1)+3. Example 3: p is cubic with table values. m(x) = -3p(x+4). Zero of m: p(x+4)=0 when x+4=2, so x=-2. AROC of m over [0,2]: m(0)=-3p(4)=-9, m(2)=-3p(6)=-21. AROC = (-21‚àí(-9))/(2-0) = -6.
dailyPhases.guidedPractice: Students compute domain and range of transformed functions. Map key points from f to g. Given table of f-values, compute g-values.
dailyPhases.independentPractice: Worksheet A-B: Find domain and range of g(x) = -2f(x‚àí1)+3 when f has domain [-3,5] and range [0,8]. Map key points. Compute transformed function values from a table.
dailyPhases.exitEvidence: If f has domain [-3,5] and range [0,8], find domain and range of g(x) = -2f(x‚àí1)+3.
dailyPhases.capReflection: What adaptability move did you make when tracking domain and range simultaneously?

dailyPhases.topicIntroduction: Complex transformations: g(x) = f(‚àíx) reflects over y-axis. Combined transformations on polynomial and rational graphs. Describe the transformation from f to g when g(x) = -f(2x+4)+1. Rewrite: g(x) = -f(2(x+2))+1. Horizontal shift left 2, horizontal compression by 1/2, reflect over x-axis, vertical shift up 1.
dailyPhases.scaffoldedExamples: Example 2: g(x) = f(‚àíx) applied to f(x) = -2(x+3)¬≤(x‚àí1). Zeros of g: x=3, x=-1. Combined transformation problem: g(x) = 4‚àí2f(5‚àíx). Domain: 5‚àíx ‚àà [-6,9] ‚Üí x ‚àà [-4,11].
dailyPhases.guidedPractice: Students work through MEGA worksheet problems: transformations across polynomial, rational, and piecewise functions.
dailyPhases.independentPractice: Worksheet C + MEGA worksheet (11 pages): comprehensive transformation practice across all function types. Sketch g(x) = ¬Ωf(x+2)+5 by mapping key points from f. Problem: match equations to transformed graphs.
dailyPhases.exitEvidence: Describe all transformations from f to g when g(x) = -f(2x+4)+1.
dailyPhases.capReflection: What courage move did you make when the MEGA worksheet combined everything you've learned?

cedTopic.title: Function Model Selection and Assumption Articulation

dailyPhases.warmUp: Baby weight data: t (weeks): 4, 5, 6, 8, 12 ‚Üí W(t) (kg): 4.2, 4.4, 4.8, 5.1, 5.7. First differences: 0.2, 0.4, 0.3, 0.6. 'Are these approximately constant?'
dailyPhases.topicIntroduction: Model selection via successive differences. Residual = y‚àí≈∑. Positive residual = model underestimates; negative = overestimates. Residual plot: no pattern = good fit; pattern = poor fit. Interpolation (within range) vs extrapolation (beyond range). Model selection framework: (1) scatter pattern, (2) successive differences, (3) function type, (4) justify in writing.
dailyPhases.scaffoldedExamples: Example 1: Baby weight data. First differences approximately constant ‚Üí linear model. W(t) = 0.186t+3.487. Predict at t=10: W(10) = 5.347 kg (interpolation). Residual at t=5: actual 4.4, predicted 4.417, residual = -0.017 (model slightly overestimates).
dailyPhases.guidedPractice: Baby that was 7.5 weeks old had residual of -0.7 kg. Model: W(t) = 0.186t+3.487. What was actual weight? Actual = Predicted + Residual = W(7.5) + (-0.7) = 4.882 - 0.7 = 4.182 kg.
dailyPhases.independentPractice: Justin Kickoff data rises then falls ‚Üí quadratic model appropriate. UK female doctors: first differences approximately constant ‚Üí linear. Compute residuals and interpret.
dailyPhases.exitEvidence: Given exponential regression output, state two assumptions the model makes about data beyond the given range.
dailyPhases.capReflection: What courage move did you make when the model did not fit perfectly?

cedTopic.title: Function Model Selection and Assumption Articulation

dailyPhases.warmUp: A residual plot shows points curving upward then downward. What does this suggest about the model type? The model is missing curvature ‚Äî a higher-degree model may be better.
dailyPhases.topicIntroduction: Model selection framework: scatter pattern ‚Üí successive differences ‚Üí function type ‚Üí justify. Amazon stock data: t: 0, 1, 13.5, 19, 26.5, 31.5, 35 ‚Üí A(t): 87.58, 124.15, 164.61, 185.97, 152.60, 122.42, 90.98. Data rises then falls ‚Üí cubic or quadratic appropriate. FRQ Task Model 2 alignment: construct model, state assumptions, predict, interpret limitations.
dailyPhases.scaffoldedExamples: Amazon stock analysis: examine rate of change pattern. Data rises to a peak around t=19 then falls. First differences go positive then negative ‚Üí cubic model captures this well. Justify model choice in writing.
dailyPhases.guidedPractice: Students justify model selection for two datasets, stating what evidence supports their choice. Residual analysis: compute residual for a given data point.
dailyPhases.independentPractice: Worksheet A-B: Model selection and assumption writing. Problem 5 (Worksheet B) aligns with FRQ Task Model 2: construct model, state assumptions, predict, interpret limitations. Model predicts value of 42.5 where actual is 39.8. Compute residual: 39.8‚àí42.5 = -2.7. Model overestimates.
dailyPhases.exitEvidence: Given a data table, which model type is most appropriate? Justify in one sentence using evidence from the data pattern.
dailyPhases.capReflection: What adaptability move did you make when two model types seemed equally plausible?

dailyPhases.warmUp: TI-84 regression steps review: enter data in L1/L2, run CubicReg, store equation in Y1. 'What does r¬≤ tell us?'
dailyPhases.topicIntroduction: Four components of every regression problem: (1) select and justify model type, (2) write regression equation, (3) predict (state interpolation or extrapolation), (4) evaluate appropriateness. Extrapolation warnings: cubic end behavior ‚Üí ¬±‚àû, unrealistic for bounded quantities like population. Always ask: does the prediction make sense in context?
dailyPhases.scaffoldedExamples: Example 1: Baby weight W(t) = 0.186t+3.487. Predict at t=10: interpolation (10 is within 4‚â§t‚â§12). Example 2: Federal Funds Rate cubic regression. Predict at t=8: extrapolation. Is this useful long-term? No ‚Äî cubic goes to ¬±‚àû but interest rates can't be arbitrarily large. Example 3: UK doctors F(t) = 1.06t+23.8. Predict for 2021 (t=33): extrapolation, but reasonable because linear trend is stable.
dailyPhases.guidedPractice: Students run regression on Federal Funds Rate data (logarithmic model) and natural gas production data (polynomial model). Interpret outputs.
dailyPhases.independentPractice: Natural gas consumption: t: 0, 10, 22, 30, 37 ‚Üí N(t): 12.4, 21.8, 20.4, 19.3, 22.6. Cubic regression. Predict for t=7 (interpolation). Amazon stock at t=16: interpolation. Actual $168.61, model predicts $159.40, residual = $9.21.
dailyPhases.exitEvidence: Given a cubic regression for a dataset, use it to predict f(10) and state one limitation of the model.
dailyPhases.capReflection: What persistence move did you make when the r¬≤ value was low?

dailyPhases.warmUp: UK doctors model: F(t) = 1.06t+23.8. Predicted percentage for 2021 (t=33): F(33) = 58.78%. Actual was 55%. Residual = 55‚àí58.78 = -3.78%. 'Does the linear model remain appropriate?'
dailyPhases.topicIntroduction: Model appropriateness evaluation. Linear extrapolation is reasonable when trend is stable and prediction isn't far beyond data range. Residual = actual‚àípredicted. Systematic residual patterns suggest model type change. Interpolation vs extrapolation reliability assessment.
dailyPhases.scaffoldedExamples: Example 3: UK doctors F(t) = 1.06t+23.8. Actual 2021: 55%. Residual: -3.78%. The model slightly overestimates but linear trend is stable ‚Äî model remains appropriate for near-term prediction. A cubic model predicts population will be negative in 2080: this tells us cubic is inappropriate for long-term extrapolation of bounded quantities.
dailyPhases.guidedPractice: Students evaluate extrapolation reliability for two scenarios: (1) linear model predicting 5 years beyond data, (2) cubic model predicting 50 years beyond data.
dailyPhases.independentPractice: Worksheet A-B: Model construction, prediction, interpretation. UK doctors ‚Üí rational function model. Evaluate extrapolation reliability. Model predicts 95,000 registered voters in 2035 from data spanning 2000-2020. Model is quadratic ‚Äî evaluate appropriateness.
dailyPhases.exitEvidence: A cubic model predicts the population will be negative in 2080. What does this tell you about the model's appropriateness?
dailyPhases.capReflection: What adaptability move did you make when evaluating whether extrapolation was reasonable?

dailyPhases.warmUp: Using the linear regression W(t) = 0.186t+3.487, predict weight at t=3 weeks. Is this interpolation or extrapolation? t=3 is below the data range [4,12] ‚Äî extrapolation below the range.
dailyPhases.topicIntroduction: FRQ 2 task model full practice: given dataset, select model type, run regression, predict, state assumptions, evaluate appropriateness. Connect modeling to polynomial and rational function features from Topics 1.1-1.12. Complete FRQ-style response with all four components.
dailyPhases.scaffoldedExamples: Full FRQ 2 practice: Amazon stock data. (1) Select cubic model (data rises then falls). (2) Run CubicReg, write equation. (3) Predict at t=16 (interpolation ‚Äî reliable) and t=50 (extrapolation ‚Äî unreliable, cubic end behavior ‚Üí ‚àí‚àû). (4) Evaluate: interpolation at t=16 is reasonable; extrapolation to t=50 is not because stock prices are bounded.
dailyPhases.guidedPractice: Students write complete FRQ 2 responses: model selection justification, regression equation, predictions with interpolation/extrapolation labels, appropriateness evaluation with reasoning.
dailyPhases.independentPractice: FRQ-style problem: given dataset, write a complete response including model selection, regression, prediction, and evaluation. Time management: practice writing under AP exam time constraints.
dailyPhases.exitEvidence: Write one sentence justifying model choice and one sentence evaluating the appropriateness of a prediction.
